title: Throwing a low serving platter
subtitle: A wide shallow thrown form with compressed floor, glazed and fired to cone 6
excerpt: A wide, low thrown platter: open the centre right out, keep the walls at 40 to 50 mm, compress the floor thoroughly, and trim a stable foot-ring at leather-hard. Glazed and fired to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Requires a potter's wheel and kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which clay holds its shape but remains damp enough to trim and join
Fully air-dried unfired clay; fragile before bisque firing
The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
The trimmed-back base ridge a finished pot sits on
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
Unfired clay in any drying stage

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.

A low serving platter is a demanding intermediate form: the wide, thin floor is prone to cracking during drying and warping during firing if the clay is not compressed thoroughly at the throwing stage. The key discipline is floor compression. Use the wooden rib to compress the floor in overlapping passes before the walls go up. Wall thickness at the base should be 8 to 10 mm, tapering slightly toward the rim. The finished platter fires to
cone
6 (1220 degrees C).

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: as needed for dipping or brushing

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 800 g to 1 kg of smooth stoneware thoroughly to remove air pockets. A platter's wide floor means any air bubble near the base will cause an explosion crack during firing. Attach a bat to the wheel head: removing a wide flat form directly from the wheel head without a bat almost always distorts it. Wet the bat lightly before centering.
Centring and opening

Centre the clay on the bat using both hands, with the wheel spinning at medium-high speed. Once centred, flatten the ball into a low, wide dome rather than the taller shape used for cylinders. Push down and outward simultaneously to spread the clay.

Open from the centre outward: push down with two or three fingers to within 8 to 10 mm of the bat surface, then drag outward to open the floor. The opening cut should reach to within 30 mm of the outer edge of the clay mass before the walls begin.

Compress the floor immediately after opening. Use the flat of the wooden rib held nearly horizontal to press down in a series of overlapping passes from the centre outward. Two passes in both directions. This closes the spiral compression marks left by the opening cut and aligns the clay particles, reducing the risk of S-cracks during drying and firing.
Raising the walls

Raise low walls by squeezing the rim clay upward with a pinching motion rather than the full pulling sequence used for a tall cylinder. The walls should reach 40 to 50 mm tall with a base wall thickness of 8 to 10 mm. Do not attempt to thin the walls below 7 mm: a thin-walled wide platter has very little structural support during firing and the rim will droop.
Refine the rim with the chamois leather, running it over the top edge to smooth and compress it. A rounded, well-compressed rim is less vulnerable to chipping in use than a sharp edge.
Use the wooden rib on the outside wall to smooth the form and compress the exterior surface. Hold the rib at a low angle against the lower third of the wall and rotate the wheel slowly.

Check the floor thickness with the needle tool: press the needle straight down through the floor at the centre and measure how much of the needle entered the clay. 8 to 10 mm is correct. If the floor is less than 6 mm thick, the platter is likely to crack during drying.

Use a sponge on a stick to remove excess water from the inside floor before cutting from the bat. Leave the platter on the bat to dry slowly. Wide flat forms benefit from a slow drying phase: cover loosely with thin plastic for the first 12 hours.
Trimming at leather-hard
When the platter has reached the
leather-hard
stage, invert it onto the banding wheel. Centre it by eye and trim a
foot-ring
using the loop tool. For a platter, a wide, shallow foot-ring approximately 6 mm tall and 10 mm wide gives stability without making the piece feel heavy when held.
Smooth the outside base and any tool marks with a damp sponge. Allow the platter to dry fully to
bone-dry
before bisque firing. Wide flat forms hold moisture longer than bowls; check the underside feels room-temperature, not cool, before firing.
Bisque firing

Load the platter flat on a kiln shelf with kiln posts to ensure good airflow. Fire slowly: a bisque ramp of 50 degrees C per hour up to 600 degrees C, then 100 degrees C per hour to cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C). A slow initial ramp drives out residual moisture without thermal shock. After the
bisque
firing, the
greenware
becomes porous and ready for glazing.
Glazing and glaze firing
Apply wax resist to the foot-ring before glazing. Glaze on the foot-ring will fuse the platter permanently to the kiln shelf.

Glaze the inside of the platter by pouring and swirling, or by brushing with a mop brush in three overlapping coats, allowing each to dry between coats. Glaze the rim and outside wall by dipping or brushing. Clean any glaze drips from the foot-ring before loading the kiln.
Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C). Allow the kiln to cool fully before opening.
Variations
Raised rim: add a second pulling pass to lift the rim to 60 mm for a deeper platter that contains sauces better.
Textured base: press a damp sponge or fabric lace against the wet floor before the walls are raised to imprint texture through the glaze.
Two-tone glaze: glaze the inside in one colour and trail a contrasting glaze across the rim for a banded look.

rows.problem: S-crack across the floor after drying
rows.cause: The floor was not compressed after opening; the spiral clay direction from opening created tension as the piece dried
rows.fix: On the next piece, compress the floor with the rib immediately after opening, using two overlapping passes. There is no repair for a dry S-crack.

rows.problem: Rim sagged or warped during glaze firing
rows.cause: Walls too thin or kiln temperature rose too quickly through the quartz inversion point at 573 degrees C
rows.fix: Keep wall thickness at 8 mm minimum. Use a slow ramp schedule through the inversion point.

rows.problem: Platter stuck to bat after throwing
rows.cause: Too little water on the bat, or the platter was cut too early before stiffening slightly
rows.fix: Leave the platter on the bat until it releases cleanly without distorting. If truly stuck, slide a thin wire cutter under it very slowly.
